detail: BSV code generation: agents need EXPLICIT distinction between typedef (compile-time type constant) and runtime value declarations (Integer, Bit#(N)). L0 natural language prompts caused agent to generate 'Integer addrWidth = 64' instead of 'typedef 64 ADDR_WIDTH'. Fix: YAML hints must explicitly state ALL constants use typedef.

BSV code generation: agents need explicit function name hints for non-obvious BSV idioms. 'split()' vs 'unpack(extend(pack()))', 'getStringPosition' return type, FShow instance pattern, tuple accessor types. Without these hints in YAML, agents guess wrong idioms.
Simple BSV typedef constants (Settings.bsv) generated correctly from L0 natural language on first attempt. T01 PASS.
Two-pass generation (generate -> compile -> fix YAML -> regenerate) is effective. PrimUtils v1->v2 fixed 11 compile errors. Headers v1->v2 fixed semantic errors. YAML hint precision is the key lever.
iter_001 complete: T01 Settings COMPILE_OK, T02 Headers COMPILE_OK (v2), T03 PrimUtils COMPILE_OK (v2). 3/3 modules compile.
Generated Settings+Headers+PrimUtils are compatible with original DataTypes.bsv (718 lines) and Utils.bsv (1788 lines). Full compilation succeeds.

mkCountCF.incrAndDecr rule has scheduling conflict: uses two separate 'if' statements instead of 'if/else if', causing both branches to write cntReg simultaneously. Fix: use 'if (incrReg[1] && !decrReg[1]) ... else if (!incrReg[1] && decrReg[1]) ...' pattern.

Standard bsc -u compilation is insufficient for correctness verification. Need -elab flag to catch rule scheduling conflicts, method conflicts, and implicit condition issues. Next compiler iteration should add elaboration check step.

mkCountCF incrAndDecr rule conflict fixed: changed two separate 'if' blocks to 'if/else if' chain. Fix validated through BSC elaboration (-elab) without scheduling conflicts and Bluesim simulation (10001 cycles, normal finish, 0 assertion failures).

Generated PrimUtils/Settings/Headers are drop-in compatible with the full 22-module blue-rdma source chain. Elaboration of mkTestPsnFunc succeeds. Full simulation passes. Original DataTypes (718 lines) and Utils (1788 lines) compile against generated modules.
BSC simulation requires: BLUESPECDIR=<bsc-install>/lib, PATH=<bsc-install>/bin first, LD_LIBRARY_PATH=<bsc-install>/lib/SAT. The bluetcl in PATH determines which BSC version runs the simulation.

iter_002 complete: T01 Settings COMPILE_OK+ELAB_OK+SIM_OK, T02 Headers COMPILE_OK+ELAB_OK+SIM_OK, T03 PrimUtils COMPILE_OK+ELAB_OK+SIM_OK. Rule conflict fixed and validated. Full chain compatibility confirmed.

Built automated compiler pipeline (compiler.py) that: validates YAML schema, assembles generation prompts from YAML knowledge (L0-L5), tracks 20 test targets, runs BSC compilation checks, and generates status reports. This is fundamentally different from iter_001/002 which relied on manual YAML editing and ad-hoc agent calls.

Agent-created YAML files exhibit structural issues: knowledge key nested inside meta instead of at top level, hint items written as dicts instead of strings, ports missing from top-level. Need stricter YAML schema validation and format enforcement in the compiler. YAML parsing also vulnerable to colons and special characters in hint text — all hint lines should be quoted.

Solution for YAML quoting issues: ALL hint lines in knowledge.bluespec_system_verilog.hints should be single-quoted strings. Lines containing # (BSV type macros), {} (code fragments), or multiple colons cause YAML parser failures when unquoted.

Compiler pipeline correctly: detects YAML parse errors, validates structure (missing meta/name/knowledge), loads multiple modules, generates prompts, runs BSC compilation, and tracks per-module status. T01 Settings -> compile_ok, T02 Headers -> compile_ok, T03 PrimUtils -> elab_ok.

iter_003 partial complete: Multi-agent pipeline architecture demonstrated. 7 YAML modules created (T01-T07). T01-T03 compile+elab verified. T04-T07 YAML has structural issues that need fixing (knowledge nesting). T04 BSV generation agent launched. ZFPR still 0.0 for full 20-target measurement but T01-T03 are verified.

JSON Schema + YAML Auto-Fixer successfully handles all known structural issues: knowledge moved from meta to top level, dict hints converted to strings, ports structure fixed. 6/6 YAML files validated. This eliminates the input quality bottleneck discovered in iter_003.

iter_004: Schema-enforced YAML generation demonstrated. 6 YAML files validated (T01-T06). Auto-fixer resolves knowledge nesting, dict hints, and structural issues. Proven T01-T03 modules verify against schema.

Built type graph extractor that parses all 22 blue-rdma source files. Key insight: Headers has 99 typedefs (our generated version is very incomplete), DataTypes has 80 typedefs + 16 enums + 27 structs. The type graph provides deterministic type context — the LLM knows exactly what types are available when generating each module. Compilation order computed by topological sort.

The fundamental bottleneck is not YAML format quality (solved in iter_004) or compiler architecture (iter_003), but the COMPLETENESS GAP between generated and original modules. Headers is 432 lines with 99 typedefs; our generated version is just a subset. This suggests the YAML→BSV approach needs either: (a) much more detailed YAML descriptions to capture all typedefs, or (b) a hybrid approach where simple typedef constants are generated deterministically and only complex logic uses LLM generation.

iter_005: Type graph extraction demonstrated. Topological compilation order computed. 22 modules analyzed with full type inventory. Key insight: completeness gap between generated and original modules is the scale bottleneck.

T04 SpecialFIFOF.bsv (669 lines, 3 modules: mkScanFIFOF, mkSearchFIFOF, mkCacheFIFO2) generated from YAML and compiles on FIRST ATTEMPT. This is the first new module beyond T01-T03 to compile. Generated by coding agent from agent-created YAML. Proves the compiler pipeline works for new modules beyond the proven T01-T03 set.

End-to-end compiler pipeline demonstrated: YAML creation (agent) → YAML fix (structural validation) → prompt assembly (compiler.py) → BSV generation (coding agent) → BSC compilation (pass). This validates the full YAML→BSV compiler architecture for modules with state machines and multiple interfaces.
Self-healing loop works: PrimUtils mkCReg type error automatically classified, YAML updated with fix hint, regenerated BSV compiles successfully. This proves the closed-loop concept.
Settings, Headers, and T04_SpecialFIFOF compile on first generation attempt from YAML. These simpler/smaller modules benefit from detailed YAML descriptions.

save_yaml() function breaks YAML format for strings containing colons. This is a known issue from iter_003/004. The self-healing loop's YAML update step must use proper quoting or a more robust serializer.
Error patterns need more coverage: mkCReg type errors and endinterface-vs-endmodule were not caught by existing regex patterns. Need to add more BSV-specific error patterns.

BSV constants at top level MUST use typedef syntax (typedef 8'h00 CONST_NAME;), not variable assignment (Type x = value;). This is a common LLM generation pitfall. Added to error classification patterns.
Phase 1 results: 4/6 compile OK (Settings, Headers, PrimUtils-healed, T04). T05 and T06 undergoing self-healing. T07-T20 have YAML parsing issues that need resolution before generation can proceed.

Self-healing through YAML regeneration can cause error whack-a-mole: fixing one issue (endinterface) introduces another (return in interface context). Five healing attempts across T05/T06 produced different errors each time.

Agent-based YAML fix generation is too imprecise for simple syntax errors. A single-line fix (change endinterface to nothing) requires restructuring the entire module generation. Future approaches need more targeted fix mechanisms.

iter_007 results: 4/6 compile (67%). Settings, Headers (1st attempt), T04 (1st attempt), PrimUtils (healed). T05, T06 failed after multiple healing attempts. Self-healing demonstrated for 1/3 error cases.

compiler.py: 400-line self-healing compiler with error classification (12 patterns), YAML healing, regeneration loop (up to 3 attempts). Key components: assemble_generation_prompt, classify_error, apply_yaml_fixes, self_healing_compile.

Multi-version voting dramatically improves per-module success rate. Settings: 3/3 compile, Headers: 3/3 compile. 6/6 individual versions compile across 2 modules (100%). Compared to iter_007 (4/6, 67%), this is a significant improvement.
With N=3 versions, P(success) goes from p to 1-(1-p)^3. Even if individual p=0.6, multi-version gives P=0.936. This leverages LLM non-determinism as a feature rather than a bug.
Multi-version voting eliminates the need for error classification and YAML healing (iter_007's main pain points). It's simpler, more parallel, and more robust. No error whack-a-mole.

Generating 3 versions per module is 3x the agent cost. For simple modules (Settings), all 3 versions compile. For complex modules, the cost is justified by higher success rate. Need a cost-aware version count strategy.

Two-stage interface-first generation started. Stage 1 generates interfaces/types only, Stage 2 generates module bodies implementing verified interfaces. PrimUtils Stage 1 generated (3884 bytes) but has expression block error on line 110.

Session produced 3 iterations (iter_007-009): self-healing (4/6 compile), multi-version voting (100% for 2 modules), two-stage generation (started). Total across all 9 iterations: 31+ findings, 7 fundamentally different directions explored.

Multi-version voting works for simple modules (Settings: 3/3, Headers: 3/3) but fails for complex modules (PrimUtils: 0/3, T04: 0/3). LLM non-determinism helps but cannot overcome fundamental BSV domain knowledge gaps for modules with rules, methods, state machines.

The effectiveness of multi-version voting correlates with module complexity. Simple typedef/struct files: 100% success. Modules with rules/methods/state: 0% success. The approach needs to be combined with better BSV domain knowledge injection for complex modules.

T05/T06 errors are structural: typedef-inside-generic-module and function-in-wrong-context. These cannot be fixed by simple YAML hint changes. Both self-healing and multi-version reach the same ceiling: LLMs lack deep BSV scoping/syntax knowledge.

Stage 1 PrimUtils uses package/endpackage wrapper with function signatures containing provisos. BSC interprets provisos clause as part of function body, expects return value. LLMs don't know this BSV edge case: function signatures with provisos in packages need different syntax than standalone files.

finding: Deterministic type extraction from original BSV eliminates type/syntax errors entirely — 4/6 modules compile with zero type fixes needed
detail: Hybrid approach (deterministic types + LLM behavior) validated: 100% type correctness for compiling modules. This is the first approach that achieves zero type errors.

finding: BSV regex-based type extraction requires careful handling: block comments (including nested), interface type definitions vs member declarations, function vs module parameter distinction, typeclass/instance internal functions
detail: Multiple edge cases discovered during implementation: leading whitespace after braces breaking regex matching, interface keyword used for both type definitions and member declarations, function keyword in module parameter lists

finding: Dependency-aware compilation order is critical for skeleton-based hybrid approach; BSC resolves imports via matching .bsv filenames (not package declarations)
detail: Arbitration and WorkCompGen fail because their imports (Utils, DataTypes, Controller) are not in the test set. Full 20-module test requires building the complete dependency chain.
